speaker: interviewee
text: This is tape 10 of 'The Survivors of the Shoah.' Interview with Itka Zygmuntowicz, March 3, 1996. So how was it at first when you were speaking in front of groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to speak-- the first time was to [? Beth ?] Jacob, a school in Philadelphia, Hebrew school. It's no longer in existence, this school. At that time, nobody told me-- I don't consider myself a speaker. I'm not an historian. I'm not a teacher. I'm just a survivor who bears witness for those whose voices have been silenced.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I didn't know even, where should I begin? What should I say? And I was always told that the truth is the easiest way. So when I came, I said the same thing I just said. I'm not a teacher. I'm not-- I'm just a survivor. And I'm not even eloquent. All I have to do is to tell you what I saw, what I thought, and what I felt. And one of the students asked me, did you ever read certain book about the Holocaust? And all kids started to laugh at him. And I felt bad for that little boy. And I said, you know what, I didn't, but I really should, because every survivor's story is different. And I started to speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from the very beginning, I realized since my life didn't begin, and fortunately didn't end through the Holocaust. So if you would tell a story, read a story, and you just hear the middle, it doesn't make sense, because you don't know what was happening this way. And this is-- so from the very beginning, I started to speak a little bit about home. And I just told a few things, mostly the story about my grandmother with the-- with the-- you only have what you give away with it, and telling me that-- to help those who are less fortunate [INAUDIBLE], and about [INAUDIBLE], and those things. And then I told a little bit above the events and spoke about the work that-- I mean, not too much in the beginning and the aftermath. Just a little bit. But people started to ask questions. And then I was asked by the Jewish Relations Council in Philadelphia-- it's called JCRC-- to join the Speaker's Bureau and to the Memorial Committee and others too, and I've been active ever since. And I belong to the Jewish Holocaust Survivors in Philadelphia, and I'm on the Memorial Committee. At one point, I was President in the ladies group. And I did a lot of other charitable work. This I don't consider charitable work. This I consider conscience, which I need to bear witness, because I survived by the very virtue. But I was also going to an old age home for several years. I used to go to the [? Graterford ?] Prison, Philadelphia, to the Jewish prisoners. And received many letters that were very insightful, because one of them said, I thought that we suffer here. And he said, we committed a crime. You didn't commit a crime, yet you have suffered so much. And I realized that it's important to bear witness, because in a short time, we won't be here, and we only have what we give away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is it hard to talk in public sometimes about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's always hard. Because when you-- where is loss, there is pain. And we lost six million, including all my loved ones, so there is pain. However, when I start talking about my separation, about my family, that becomes even more, so sometimes I try to avoid it. But people want to know about it, because this is the path. So there is pain, but whether I talk about it-- the same as like the loss of my son-- whether I talk about it or don't, the pain is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at least I do something with it. Like now, God willing, April 14th, a day before my birthday, I'm supposed-- scheduled to go, if God wills it, to Poland, back to Auschwitz, and to Israel on the march of the living.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's going to be, I visualize, extremely hard to go to the place where my loved ones, so many were murdered. But, since going with young people, with students, they will benefit something. So at least it won't be just pain, it will be beneficial. I'll have what I'll give away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm supposed to go a week here. And to know the very fact that I was marching from Auschwitz to Israel, that we have a homeland after 2,000 years, for those who couldn't do it-- it's extremely meaningful for me. But it's going to be very important to me, too. So I never shied away, you know? People are afraid to talk about hurt, about pain. But when you don't talk, it's even worse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Have you been interviewed locally about your experiences a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- never in-depth. Never in-depth. Because whenever you're interviewed, it's 20 minutes here, a half an hour here. You never have a chance to-- to go in that much, you know? I was once-- but I was many times on television and the newspaper. I wrote, you know, poems and a story. In fact-- I don't know if it's time, but there is-- there is one poem that when I was on television, I read, and they got a lot of responses. Many people wanted this poem. It's been made now to a song, too. But right now, I don't think I'm going to sing it. Or maybe I should, I don't know. It's called 'Show Your Face,' and it was written in 1978. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SINGING] Show your face-- no, I think I'm going to-- I'm going to-- I'm too exhausted now to sing it. Show your face at least in a dream. I wake up in terror and hear your screams. Your images I see in ashes and flames, yet still, I can hear you calling my name. Show your face. I'm so alone. My father and mother and brothers are gone. They left me behind to carry the pain and to see that their death shall not be in vain. Show your face so my children can see the roots of my [? chopped-up ?] family tree. Heal the branches with your faith and love, until we are united in heaven up above.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. Thank you. This picture-- on this picture is my great-grandmother [? Zita ?] and her son. My great-grandmother [? Zita ?] was [INAUDIBLE]. She was-- she was born in Poland, and this is her son.

speaker: interviewee
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it's [INAUDIBLE], because she-- she had three sons and four daughters. But I'm not quite sure which one this is. But I know that she was very Orthodox, and all of the children were Orthodox, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in Ciechanow, Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: About when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime in 1800. And there is a story how I got all the pictures. In-- Until 1984, I had no relatives here from-- all my relatives from Poland, that were in Poland didn't survive, except on my father's side, that were in Russia. In 1984, I received a letter from Ida [? Jarvis, ?] Fall's Church, Washington. I had no idea. The name didn't mean anything. I never heard it before. And when I opened it, it had a note. It said, 'I'm your cousin. We are named after the same great-grandmother, [? Zita ?] [? Shinekopf. ?] My family immigrated when I was four years old to America, and I found a photograph of your great-grandmother-- grandmother'-- not this photograph, but of my grandmother. This is my great-grandmother, and I'm sure you would like to have it.' And I appreciated it very much. I thanked her. And she invited me to come to see her. When I came there, she told me that two of the cousins which came here before the Holocaust organized that every year they should have a Thanksgiving reunion, because people were living in different places.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we'll show that picture in a few minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And she-- This picture I received from Ida [? Jarvis ?] when she sent me the letter in 1984. On this picture, you see my grandmother-- my maternal, widowed grandmother, Chaye Sure Zebrah, who lived with us in Ciechanow, Poland. And her son, [? Moshe ?] [INAUDIBLE] Zebrah, who emigrated from Poland to London, England about when he was 18 years old. And my grandmother went to visit him, and this is when this picture was done.

speaker: interviewee
text: When was it taken, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in the beginning of 1900 sometime.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In London, England.

speaker: interviewee
text: About how old is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I have no idea. I know she lived to have-- a long time, but I don't know exactly how old she was. She had five married children and-- This is a picture of my late maternal grandfather. His name was Isaac Zebrah. I was not privileged to know him, because he was no longer alive when I was born. And I received this, too, from [? Ida ?] and her relatives.

speaker: interviewee
text: When was this taken, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea, but must also been sometime in the late 1800s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, yes. Because he was born, lived, and died in Poland. On this picture, you see, on the right side-- right side, my mother, Simo Frajman, and her first cousin. They were both born in Poland. I didn't know the cousin. I don't think I was alive yet when this was taken. I have no idea what date, but I know it's in Poland. It's my mother's first cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: About how old is your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. But she was pretty young at that time. This-- On this picture are three of my first cousins. On the right side, standing, is my cousin Hannah Pazdziorek. The other one next to her on the left is [? Ito ?] Pazdziorek. And the one sitting down-- I mean, is [? Luza ?] Pazdziorek. And the one sitting down is [? Ito ?] Pazdziorek, who emigrated in 1934 to Israel when I was four years old. And the picture was taken in 1930 in Ciechanow, Poland. This photograph is of my beloved parents of blessed memory. My father, Eljo Frajman, my mother, Simo Frajman, and I, Itka Frajman Zygmuntowicz. I was seven years old. I got this picture actually twice. It's a composite picture. I got a separate picture of my mother and a separate picture of my father and I. The first time I got this picture was from my uncle, [? Moshe ?] [INAUDIBLE] Zebrah, from England, when he find out I'm alive. The second time, I got from my kindergarten teacher, that my father gave in 1939, beginning of 1939, before she left.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was [? Malka ?] [INAUDIBLE], and she lives in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Ciechanow, Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was seven years old. I was born in 19-- in 1926. [INAUDIBLE]. This photograph I got first was cut out. The one on the bottom row to the left, the cutout picture, I got in Israel when I went in 1978. The second time I came, they gave me the rest of the picture. This was taken in [? Pultusk, ?] Poland, when my mother was playing theater. And later on, I got the other part.

speaker: interviewee
text: And who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is her-- the people who played with her in the Yiddish theatre.

speaker: interviewee
text: With who?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother, Simo Frajman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1925. It was before I was born. On the picture on top, to the left, you will see the Kalfus family. In the middle are the parents, Mr. And Mrs. Kalfus. On the side are the two brothers, which live in Israel and have now families themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just know one, the one that-- Kalfus, that I met, that you saw previously the picture. And there is also a little sister. I don't know what's happened to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: What's the one that you know's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Joshua ?] Kalfus.

speaker: interviewee
text: And where is this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken Ciechanow. In the background, you see the building, the school to which I went, which he owned.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the picture was taken? I'm not quite sure. I'm not quite sure. [INAUDIBLE] historic. This picture is of [? Joshua ?] Kalfus, the one that I mentioned that survived because of my mother's sacrifice and mine during the Holocaust when we were interrogated. And it was taken in-- this picture was taken in 1948. I got the picture when I came to the World Gathering of Jewish Holocaust Survivors, and I met [? Joshua ?] Kalfus in 1981.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened to his parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: His parents perished in Auschwitz, too-- were murdered in Auschwitz, just like my family. This picture is taken in Sweden, in [? Ravlanda, ?] when we worked in the hospital and [INAUDIBLE]. And it's on this picture, you see to the-- to the right is I, Itka Zygmuntowicz Frajman. And then it's Bina [? Goldcome ?] at that time, and now-- I mean [? Janka ?] [? Goldcome ?] and then Bina [? Clook. ?] It's the same person, but this is when she was married. Her name is [? Clook ?] now. And [? Regina ?] Shapirow. We were all at that time working in [? Ravlanda. ?] And this was taken in 1945. No, no-- '45, yes. 1945. On this photograph is my husband, Rachmil Zygmuntowicz. In the middle is our oldest grandson, Ezra Zygmuntowicz, and myself, Itka Zygmuntowicz Frajman.

speaker: interviewee
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: When was it taken? This was taken this year, actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? In front of our home in Philadelphia. [INAUDIBLE]. This is my second grandson, Eli-- Eli Zygmuntowicz. And this was taken in 1995. This is my youngest granddaughter. She is named-- her middle-- her middle name is after my late son, Michael Zygmuntowicz. Her name [INAUDIBLE] in Hebrew is [? Michaela. ?] And her first-- she has two names-- [? Elizabeth ?] Michaela, and we call her [? Eliza. ?] When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: When was it taken? Last year--

speaker: interviewee
text: How old is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: --1995. She's not even two now, so it must have been like a year and a half or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in New York, where they live in New York City. This is a photograph of my new-found relatives that originate-- they came from Poland, but they live now in Philadelphia, that I wasn't aware of. And my cousin, [? Ida ?] Jarvis invited me. They make every year a family reunion. It's called the [? Goodstein ?] Family Reunion. And in this picture, I found, all of a sudden, from not having Canada relatives, all those relatives. And we try every year on Thanksgiving to get together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when was it-- where-- when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one is in 1984, the first reunion that we were in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Washington. And my family's-- my family's on the-- don't have the entire family there, but my family's on the right-hand side in the middle, someplace.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph I got when I went to the Jewish Holocaust Survivors Reunion in 1981 in Jerusalem. And at that time, my cousin's husband was still alive, and he gave me this photograph. And this is my entire family-- my mother's older sister. And from the left, the first row, is [? Hannah, ?] the other is [? Moshe, ?] then is [? Yiddis, ?] and then is my cousin [? Ziso's ?] husband, and then is [? Rujo. ?] Then, going back from the right to the left--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in the front row now?

speaker: interviewee
text: The front row is my cousin [? Ziso, ?] then it's my aunt [? Ester ?] Teel, and my uncle, Shmuel [INAUDIBLE] Pazdziorek. And this is their whole family. They lived in our hometown, Ciechanow, Poland. We were very close.

speaker: interviewee
text: And next to your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to my uncle is my cousin, the youngest daughter, [? Hannah. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And their last names are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pazdziorek, And this was my cousin [? Ito's ?] family. And they all perished, except my cousin [? Ito, ?] who had left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in-- it must have been taken in-- in the 1930s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ciechanow, Poland, also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
